title: The Crew: The Quest for Planet Nine

walkthrough.title: Find the Card Deck
walkthrough.text: Let's get The Crew set up! First, find the deck of forty large playing cards. This includes thirty-six color cards in four suits (blue, green, yellow, and pink, each numbered one through nine) and four rocket cards numbered one through four. These rockets are the trump suit.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all forty cards and deal them face down as evenly as possible to all players. In a four-player game, each player gets exactly ten cards. In a three-player game, one player will have fourteen cards and two will have thirteen. In a five-player game, each player gets eight cards.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Tokens
walkthrough.text: Give each player one radio communication token with the green side face up. Place the distress signal token face down nearby. Also make sure each player has a reminder card handy. Shuffle the thirty-six small task cards into a face-down deck in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Find the Commander
walkthrough.text: Everyone looks at their hand. The player holding Rocket four is the Commander for this mission. That player takes the Commander token. The Commander will lead the first trick and choose the first task card during task distribution. If you are playing Mission one, you are ready to begin.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Mission
walkthrough.text: Open the logbook and find your current mission number. Each mission tells you how many task cards to reveal. Flip that many task cards face up in the center. The Commander picks their task card first, then drafting continues clockwise, one task at a time, until all tasks are assigned. Now you are ready to play.

summary.title: Find the Card Deck
bullets: 40 large cards: 36 color cards (4 suits × 9) + 4 rocket cards
Color suits: blue, green, yellow, pink (1-9 each)
Rockets: numbered 1-4, serve as trump suit

bullets: Shuffle all 40 cards, deal evenly
3 players: 14/13/13, 4 players: 10 each, 5 players: 8 each

summary.title: Distribute Tokens
bullets: Each player: 1 radio token (green side up) + 1 reminder card
Place distress signal face down nearby
Shuffle 36 task cards into face-down deck

summary.title: Find the Commander
bullets: Rocket 4 holder = Commander
Commander takes Commander token
Commander leads first trick and picks first task

summary.title: Set Up the Mission
bullets: Check logbook for mission number → reveals task count
Commander picks first task → clockwise draft
All tasks assigned → ready to play

walkthrough.title: The Core Concept
walkthrough.text: The Crew is a cooperative trick-taking game. Your team must complete missions by winning specific cards in specific tricks, assigned to specific players. If the right player wins their assigned task card, the task is complete. If the wrong player wins it, the mission fails immediately. You will play through fifty missions of increasing difficulty.

walkthrough.title: Playing a Trick
walkthrough.text: The Commander leads the first trick by playing any card from their hand. Going clockwise, each player must follow suit if they can. If you have a card of the led suit, you must play it. If you cannot follow suit, you may play any card. The highest card of the led suit wins the trick, unless a rocket card was played. Rockets beat everything, and the highest rocket wins among rockets.

walkthrough.title: Winning Tricks
walkthrough.text: The player who wins a trick takes all the cards and places them face down in front of themselves. Then that player leads the next trick. Tricks continue until all cards have been played. In a three-player game, one card remains unplayed after the final trick.

walkthrough.title: Task Cards
walkthrough.text: Task cards are the heart of each mission. Each small task card matches a specific large playing card. When a task card is assigned to you, it means you must personally win the trick that contains that matching playing card. If someone else wins it instead, the mission fails. One trick can complete multiple tasks if they belong to the same player.

walkthrough.title: Communication Rules
walkthrough.text: Here is the challenge: you cannot talk about your hand. No hints, no suggestions, no signals. Your only communication tool is the radio. Once per mission attempt, each player may use their radio communication token to reveal one color card from their hand by placing it face up. Then position your token on the card to indicate if it is your highest card of that suit, your only card of that suit, or your lowest card of that suit. You cannot communicate rocket cards.

walkthrough.title: Communication Timing
walkthrough.text: You can only communicate before a trick begins, never during one. Place your color card face up and position the token at the top, middle, or bottom of the card. Top means it is your highest of that color. Middle means it is your only card of that color. Bottom means it is your lowest. Once that card is played in a trick, flip your token to the red side to show you have used your signal.

walkthrough.title: Mission Success and Failure
walkthrough.text: A mission succeeds when all task cards have been completed by their assigned owners and all mission-specific rules are followed. A mission fails when the wrong player wins a required card or a mission-specific rule is broken. On failure, reshuffle all cards, redeal, redistribute tasks, and try again. On success, move to the next mission in the logbook.

walkthrough.title: Special Mission Rules
walkthrough.text: As you progress, missions add special constraints. Some require tasks to be completed in a specific order, marked by numbered tokens. Others restrict communication further or add other conditions. The logbook describes each mission's unique rules. Always read the mission description before setting up tasks.

summary.title: The Core Concept
bullets: Cooperative trick-taking: win specific cards with specific players
Wrong player wins a task card → mission fails immediately
50 missions of escalating difficulty

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Mission One
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first mission in The Crew! We will walk through Mission one with four players. Mission one is simple: there is just one task card to complete. This tutorial will teach you trick-taking, task completion, and communication. Let's launch!

walkthrough.title: Deal and Find the Commander
walkthrough.text: Shuffle and deal ten cards to each player. Player three has Rocket four, so they are the Commander. Player three takes the Commander token. One task card is revealed: let's say it is Blue seven. The Commander must pick it since it is the only task, so Blue seven is now Player three's responsibility.

walkthrough.title: First Trick: The Commander Leads
walkthrough.text: Player three leads with Blue five. Player four has Blue two and must follow suit, playing Blue two. Player one has no blue cards and plays Yellow three. Player two plays Blue eight. Player two wins the trick with the highest blue card. Player two leads next.

walkthrough.title: Why Following Suit Matters
walkthrough.text: Notice that Player one could not follow suit, so they played a yellow card. That yellow card cannot win the trick because blue was led. Only blue cards or rockets can win a blue-led trick. Understanding when you are void in a suit and can play off-suit is crucial for directing cards to the right player.

walkthrough.title: Second Trick: Setting Up the Task
walkthrough.text: Player two leads with Green six. Players follow suit where they can. Player three has no green and plays Blue seven into the trick. But wait, Player three cannot win this trick because green was led and Blue seven is not the led suit. Blue seven will go to whoever wins this green trick. Is that Player three? No! This is a problem.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Wasting Your Task Card
walkthrough.text: Player three just threw away Blue seven into a trick they cannot win. Now whoever wins this green trick will get Blue seven, and if it is not Player three, the mission fails! This is the most common beginner mistake: playing your task card when you cannot win the trick. Never play a task card off-suit unless you are certain you will win with a rocket.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Learning from the Error
walkthrough.text: Let's rewind. The mission fails because Player four wins the green trick containing Blue seven. Player three learns an important lesson: hold onto your task card until you can win the trick yourself. Keep Blue seven in hand and wait for a blue trick, or lead blue yourself when you have the highest blue card. Let's reshuffle and try the mission again.

walkthrough.title: Second Attempt: Better Strategy
walkthrough.text: New deal. Player three is Commander again with Rocket four. The same task comes up: Blue seven assigned to Player three. This time, Player three leads with a low blue card to test the waters. Other players follow suit. Player three takes note of which blue cards come out.

walkthrough.title: Using Communication
walkthrough.text: Before the third trick, Player one decides to communicate. They place Blue nine face up and put their token at the top, meaning this is their highest blue card. Now everyone knows Player one has Blue nine, the highest possible blue card. Player three knows that if they lead blue, Player one will likely win. Player three needs to be careful about when to lead blue.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Wasting Communication
walkthrough.text: Player four communicates Yellow two with the token at the bottom, meaning it is their lowest yellow. This information is nearly useless for the current mission. The lesson: save your one communication signal for information that actually helps complete a task. Telling people about your lowest yellow card when the only task is Blue seven does not help anyone.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Communicate High-Impact Information
walkthrough.text: Your communication token is precious. Use it only when the information changes the team's plan. Signaling that you hold a card relevant to a task, or that you are void in a critical suit, is far more valuable than sharing random hand information. Think before you signal.

walkthrough.title: Completing the Mission
walkthrough.text: Player one leads blue later in the round. Player three follows with Blue seven. Player one plays Blue nine and wins the trick, but that is fine because Player three already played Blue seven into a trick they need to win. Wait, no! Player three needs to win the trick with Blue seven in it. Let's say instead that Player three leads with Rocket four, the highest trump. They play Blue seven off-suit earlier, then use Rocket four to win a trick where Blue seven appears. Actually, the simplest path: Player three leads Blue seven when they think they have the highest blue remaining, and wins the trick. Mission complete!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, crew! You have completed Mission one of The Crew. You learned how trick-taking works, experienced the pain of playing a task card at the wrong time, and saw how communication can help or be wasted. The missions only get more challenging from here, with more tasks and tighter constraints.

walkthrough.title: Before Your Next Mission
walkthrough.text:
Before you launch into Mission two, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for trick management and communication strategy.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to suits, trumps, and mission types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Preserve Your Rockets
walkthrough.text: Rockets are the trump suit and can win any trick. It is tempting to use them to win easy tricks early, but that leaves you without rescue options later. Save your rockets for must-win task moments when you absolutely need to guarantee a trick. Spending Rocket three on a casual trick in the first round means you cannot use it to save a critical task later.

walkthrough.title: Lead Control Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Whoever wins a trick chooses what suit to lead next. This is incredibly powerful. If you need to complete a blue task, winning a trick gives you the lead so you can play blue. Think of trick-winning not just as collecting cards, but as gaining the right to choose the next suit. Sometimes losing a trick on purpose is the best play if it gives lead to a teammate who needs it.

walkthrough.title: Map Your Task Targets Early
walkthrough.text: As soon as tasks are distributed, look at your hand and figure out your path to completing each task. Which suits are you strong in? Which are you void in? Can you win the task card directly, or do you need a teammate to feed it to you? Planning before the first trick is played saves you from reactive mistakes.

walkthrough.title: Communicate Strategically
walkthrough.text: Your one communication per mission is precious. Do not use it in the first trick unless the information is urgent. The best time to communicate is when you hold a card that directly affects a task and the team needs to know about it. Signaling your highest card in the task's suit, or that you are void in a critical suit, changes how the whole team plays.

walkthrough.title: Watch for Wrong-Owner Danger
walkthrough.text: The biggest threat in The Crew is the wrong player accidentally winning a task card. Before playing a card into a trick, ask yourself: could this trick contain someone else's task card? If so, consider whether winning this trick would cause a mission failure. Sometimes the best play is to deliberately lose a trick.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Lead Intentionally
walkthrough.text: Do not win tricks by accident. Every trick you win gives you the lead, and leading carelessly can force bad situations. If you have no tasks left to complete and another player does, consider playing low to pass the lead to them. Winning unnecessary tricks is one of the most common mistakes in The Crew.

walkthrough.title: Three-Player Special Considerations
walkthrough.text: In a three-player game, one card goes unplayed. This means a task card could be trapped in the unplayed pile and can never be won. If a task seems impossible, this might be why. Also, the three-player challenge variant removes all green cards and Rocket one for a tighter, harder experience.

bullets: Rockets trump everything — save for must-win moments
Don't waste on casual tricks
Rocket in reserve = rescue option later

bullets: Winning a trick → choose next suit to lead

Losing on purpose → pass lead to a teammate who needs it

walkthrough.title: Suit Void Management
walkthrough.text: Being void in a suit is one of the most powerful positions in The Crew. When you have no cards of a suit, you can play anything into that trick, including a rocket to steal a task card or a low off-suit card to dump unwanted cards. Deliberately voiding yourself by playing out all cards of a suit early can set up game-winning plays later.

walkthrough.title: Task Ordering Strategy
walkthrough.text: In missions with numbered task tokens requiring specific completion order, work backward from the final task. Which cards need to be played last? Those suits should be saved. Which tasks need to happen first? Prioritize those leads. The Commander's task drafting should consider not just which tasks they can complete, but the overall sequence the team needs.

walkthrough.title: Reading Communication Precisely
walkthrough.text: When a teammate communicates, extract maximum information. If they show Green five at the top (highest green), that tells you they have no green card above five. That means Green six through nine are held by other players or undealt. This kind of deductive reasoning from a single signal is what separates good crews from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Ducking and Feeding
walkthrough.text: Ducking means intentionally playing low to avoid winning a trick. Feeding means leading a suit specifically so a teammate can win. These are complementary techniques. If Player two has a blue task and you have the lead, feed them by leading blue with a low card. If a trick contains someone else's task card, duck by playing your lowest card so you do not accidentally win it.

walkthrough.title: Five-Player Task Handover
walkthrough.text: From Mission twenty-five onward in five-player games, one crew member may hand over a task card to another before the first trick. This is incredibly powerful. Give a task to the player whose hand best supports completing it. Discuss who gives and receives without revealing hand information. This mechanic exists because five-player games are the hardest difficulty.

walkthrough.title: Late-Mission Discipline
walkthrough.text: The hardest missions have five or more tasks with ordering constraints. Discipline is everything. Do not play impulsively. Before each trick, mentally check: does this play risk any task failure? Is the ordering constraint still safe? Can I still complete my remaining tasks with my remaining cards? Slow, deliberate play wins more missions than rushed cleverness.

summary.title: Suit Void Management
bullets: Being void in a suit = play anything (rockets or dumps)
Deliberately void yourself by playing out a suit early
Void position enables game-winning plays

summary.title: Task Ordering Strategy
bullets: Work backward from final ordered task
Save suits needed for late tasks
Commander drafts tasks considering team sequence

summary.title: Reading Communication Precisely
bullets: Green 5 at top → no green above 5 in their hand
Deduce where remaining high cards are
One signal can reveal a lot with deductive reasoning

summary.title: Ducking and Feeding
bullets: Duck: play low to avoid winning unwanted tricks
Feed: lead a suit for a teammate's task completion
Complementary techniques for task management

summary.title: Five-Player Task Handover
bullets: Mission 25+ (5 players): 1 task can be transferred

summary.title: Late-Mission Discipline

Slow deliberate play > rushed cleverness

entries.definition: Thirty-six cards in four suits (blue, green, yellow, pink), each numbered one through nine. You must follow the led suit if you hold a card of that suit.
Four trump cards numbered one through four. Rockets beat all color cards. The highest rocket wins among rockets. Rocket four determines the Commander.
Thirty-six small cards matching each color card. When assigned to you, you must personally win the trick containing the matching playing card.
Each player gets one token per mission attempt. Used to reveal one color card face up with position indicating highest, only, or lowest of that suit. Cannot communicate rocket cards.
The player holding Rocket four. The Commander takes the Commander token, leads the first trick, and picks the first task card during distribution.
A token that can be used in certain missions to modify difficulty. Check the logbook for when and how it applies to your current mission.

If the led suit is blue and you have blue cards, you must play a blue card. You are not required to play a higher card, just a card of the same suit. If you have no cards of the led suit, you may play any card.
Rocket cards function as the trump suit. A rocket played into any trick beats all color cards. If multiple rockets are played, the highest-numbered rocket wins.
One round of play where each player contributes one card. The leader plays first, then play continues clockwise. The highest card of the led suit (or highest rocket) wins.
Playing the first card of a trick. The leader sets the suit that other players must follow. The Commander leads the first trick; subsequent trick leaders are the previous trick's winners.
Intentionally playing a low card to avoid winning a trick. Used when the trick contains another player's task card that you should not capture.
Leading a specific suit to help a teammate win the trick and complete their task. For example, leading blue when a teammate needs to win a blue task card.
Numbered tokens placed on task cards to indicate required completion order. Task one must be completed before task two, and so on.
A specific challenge from the logbook defining how many tasks to complete and any special rules. The game includes fifty missions of increasing difficulty.
A simulated third player used in two-player games. The Commander manages JARVIS by choosing which face-up cards to play from a fourteen-card tableau.
